title: Rust vs TypeScript
description: Side-by-side comparison for TypeScript developers learning Rust

comparisons.rust:
let x = 5;          // Immutable by default
let mut y = 10;      // Mutable binding
const Z: i32 = 15;  // Compile-time constant
comparisons.note: Rust's `let` is like TS `const`. Rust's `const` is for compile-time constants only (not just immutable bindings).
Rust has distinct numeric types (i32, f64, etc.) instead of a single `number` type. Closures use || syntax.
Rust traits are like interfaces but can also provide default implementations and are used for operator overloading.
Rust separates data (struct) from behavior (impl). No inheritance — use traits and composition instead.
TS generics are erased at runtime. Rust generics are monomorphized (specialized code generated per type) — zero runtime cost.
Rust enums are algebraic data types — each variant can carry different data. They are much more powerful than TS enums.
Rust has no exceptions. Result<T, E> makes errors part of the type system, so you cannot accidentally ignore them.
Rust Futures are lazy — they do nothing until polled. JS Promises start executing immediately. Note .await comes after the expression.
Rust also has fixed-size arrays: `let arr: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3]`. Vec is heap-allocated and growable like JS arrays.
Both return an optional type for lookups. Rust uses Option<&V> instead of T | undefined.

comparisons.typescript:
const s: string = "hello";
const template = `Hello, ${name}!`;
comparisons.note: Rust has two main string types: &str (borrowed, like a view) and String (owned, heap-allocated). TS has only one string type.
Rust enums serve the same purpose as TS discriminated unions but are more ergonomic and enforced by the compiler.

comparisons.typescript: const name = user?.profile?.name ?? "Unknown";
comparisons.rust:
let name = user
    .as_ref()
    .and_then(|u| u.profile.as_ref())
    .map(|p| p.name.as_str())
    .unwrap_or("Unknown");
comparisons.note: Rust uses Option combinators instead of ?. chaining. It is more verbose but prevents any null-related runtime errors.
Rust match is exhaustive — the compiler errors if you miss a variant. TS narrowing is more manual.
Rust uses `mod` to declare modules and `pub` for visibility. File structure maps to module hierarchy.
Rust async functions return impl Future. You need a runtime (tokio) to execute them. TS uses the built-in event loop.
Rust has built-in testing with #[test]. No external test runner needed. Run with `cargo test`.
Rust has no null or undefined. Option<T> is either Some(value) or None. The compiler forces you to handle both cases.
Both languages support destructuring. Rust uses `..` to ignore remaining fields and `@` for binding sub-patterns.
Rust iterators are lazy — they do nothing until consumed (with collect, sum, etc.). JS array methods execute eagerly.
